edges.description: Reduced PRS-I activity limits PRPP-dependent nucleotide biosynthesis.
PRPS1 loss of function is directly observed as reduced or undetectable PRS-I enzyme activity in patient-derived assay systems.
Reduced nucleotide availability is associated with neurologic, auditory, and ocular manifestations across the PRS deficiency continuum.
Severe PRPS1 deficiency can include recurrent respiratory infections and T-cell dysfunction that respond to pathway precursor supplementation.
Some severe PRPS1 loss-of-function presentations include growth, endocrine, white-matter, and retinal findings beyond the classic three subtype clusters.
Impaired purine biosynthesis is reflected by undetectable urinary hypoxanthine in affected patients.
Impaired purine biosynthesis is reflected by reduced serum uric acid in affected patients.
Severe PRPS1 deficiency can lower erythrocyte guanine nucleotide pools.
Auditory vulnerability manifests as sensorineural hearing loss across the PRPS1 deficiency continuum.
Neurologic vulnerability includes peripheral neuropathy in CMTX5 and severe Arts syndrome presentations.
Arts syndrome neurologic involvement includes early-onset ataxia.
Severe PRPS1 deficiency produces developmental delay as part of the neurologic phenotype.
Severe neurologic involvement includes delayed motor development.
Severe neurologic involvement includes intellectual disability.
Severe neurologic involvement includes early-onset hypotonia.
Ocular vulnerability includes progressive optic atrophy.
Ocular vulnerability can include retinal dystrophy in the progressive ophthalmologic spectrum.
Immune vulnerability in severe Arts syndrome manifests as recurrent severe respiratory infections.
Infection-associated clinical worsening in Arts syndrome can include muscle weakness.
Severe expanded-spectrum PRPS1 deficiency includes prenatal growth restriction.
Severe expanded-spectrum PRPS1 deficiency includes spastic quadriparesis, represented by spastic tetraparesis.
Severe expanded-spectrum PRPS1 deficiency includes white matter disease.
Severe expanded-spectrum PRPS1 deficiency includes diabetes insipidus.
Severe expanded-spectrum PRPS1 deficiency includes severe short stature.
Severe expanded-spectrum PRPS1 deficiency includes dysmorphic facial features.

edges.source: Purine and NAD Pathway Precursor Supplementation

edges.description: Reduced PRS-I enzyme activity directly reports the proximal PRPS1 loss-of-function mechanism.
Undetectable urinary hypoxanthine reports impaired purine biosynthesis downstream of PRPP deficiency.
Reduced serum uric acid reports impaired purine biosynthesis downstream of PRPP deficiency.
Low erythrocyte GTP and GDP report impaired nucleotide availability in severe PRPS1 deficiency.

nodes.description: Ataxia is a neurologic feature of Arts syndrome and is also described as a milder later-onset manifestation in the PRS deficiency continuum.

evidence.snippet: Arts syndrome is an X-linked disorder characterized by mental retardation, early-onset hypotonia, ataxia, delayed motor development, hearing impairment, and optic atrophy.
Arts syndrome is characterized by early-onset hypotonia, ataxia, intellectual disability, sensorineural hearing impairment, progressive optic atrophy, and a tendency to develop infections.

nodes.description: Diabetes insipidus has been reported in severe expanded-spectrum PRPS1 deficiency.

evidence.snippet: Additional phenotypic features include macular coloboma-like lesions with retinal dystrophy, severe short stature and diabetes insipidus.

nodes.description: Dysmorphic facial features are reported in severe expanded-spectrum PRPS1 deficiency presentations.

evidence.reference_title: Prenatal growth restriction, retinal dystrophy, diabetes insipidus and white matter disease: expanding the spectrum of PRPS1-related disorders.
evidence.snippet: intrauterine growth restriction, dysmorphic facial features, severe intellectual disability and spastic quadraparesis.
Nucleotide analysis in erythrocytes revealed abnormally low guanosine triphosphate and guanosine diphosphate.

readouts.interpretation: Low erythrocyte GTP and GDP report impaired nucleotide availability in severe PRPS1 deficiency.

nodes.description: Rare severe PRPS1 deficiency presentations can include intrauterine growth restriction, severe short stature, diabetes insipidus, spastic quadriparesis, white matter disease, retinal dystrophy, and low erythrocyte guanine nucleotides.

evidence.snippet: intrauterine growth restriction, dysmorphic facial features, severe intellectual disability and spastic quadraparesis.
Additional phenotypic features include macular coloboma-like lesions with retinal dystrophy, severe short stature and diabetes insipidus.

nodes.description: Developmental delay in PRPS1 deficiency includes delayed motor development and broader developmental impairment, especially in severe Arts syndrome.

evidence.snippet: severe congenital profound sensorineural hearing loss, intellectual disability, delayed motor development, and progressive ophthalmologic involvement

Arts syndrome, or severe PRPS1 deficiency, is an X-linked condition characterized by congenital sensorineural hearing loss, optic atrophy, developmental delays, ataxia, hypotonia, and recurrent infections that can cause progressive clinical decline

nodes.description: Early-onset muscular hypotonia is a characteristic severe Arts syndrome feature and can worsen with recurrent infections.

evidence.snippet: Arts syndrome is an X-linked disorder characterized by mental retardation, early-onset hypotonia, ataxia, delayed motor development, hearing impairment, and optic atrophy.
The initial symptoms of the 1-year-old proband were hypotonia and ataxia, worsening recurrent infection-triggered muscle weakness, motor and intellectual developmental delay, and hearing loss.

nodes.description: Severe Arts syndrome includes recurrent, often respiratory, infections. Co-therapy evidence suggests that impaired purine and NAD-pathway support contributes to T-cell survival and functional defects.

evidence.snippet: Classically, affected males present with sensorineural hearing loss, optic atrophy, muscular hypotonia, developmental impairment, and recurrent severe respiratory infections early in life.
SAM and nicotinamide riboside co-therapy further improved his clinical phenotype as well as T-cell survival and function.

nodes.description: PRS-I produces PRPP, which is used for purine, pyrimidine, and NAD-pathway nucleotide synthesis. PRPS1 loss of function therefore decreases nucleotide biosynthetic capacity and produces measurable purine and guanine nucleotide abnormalities in patients.

evidence.snippet: This enzyme generates phospho-ribosyl-pyrophosphate (PRPP), which is utilized in the synthesis of purines, nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD), and NAD phosphate (NADP), among other metabolic pathways.
Nucleotide analysis in erythrocytes revealed abnormally low guanosine triphosphate and guanosine diphosphate.

nodes.description: Intellectual disability is part of severe Arts syndrome and may be severe in expanded PRPS1-deficiency presentations.

evidence.snippet: Arts syndrome is characterized by early-onset hypotonia, ataxia, intellectual disability, sensorineural hearing impairment, progressive optic atrophy, and a tendency to develop infections.
intrauterine growth restriction, dysmorphic facial features, severe intellectual disability and spastic quadraparesis.

nodes.description: Intrauterine growth restriction is described in severe expanded-spectrum PRPS1 deficiency.

evidence.snippet: intrauterine growth restriction, dysmorphic facial features, severe intellectual disability and spastic quadraparesis.

nodes.description: Motor developmental delay is a core severe Arts syndrome manifestation.

evidence.snippet: Arts syndrome is an X-linked disorder characterized by mental retardation, early-onset hypotonia, ataxia, delayed motor development, hearing impairment, and optic atrophy.

nodes.description: There is no cure for PRS deficiency, so management includes coordinated neurologic, rehabilitation, hearing, vision, education, and genetics care to maximize function and reduce complications.

evidence.snippet: There is no cure for PRS deficiency. Supportive care to improve quality of life, maximize function, and reduce complications can include multidisciplinary care by specialists in neurology, physiatry, physical therapy, occupational therapy, audiology, otolaryngology, ophthalmology and low vision services, education, and medical genetics.

nodes.description: Muscle weakness, often triggered or worsened by recurrent infections, is described in Arts syndrome alongside hypotonia and ataxia.

evidence.reference_title: Arts syndrome with a novel missense mutation in the PRPS1 gene: A case report.
evidence.snippet: The initial symptoms of the 1-year-old proband were hypotonia and ataxia, worsening recurrent infection-triggered muscle weakness, motor and intellectual developmental delay, and hearing loss.

nodes.description: PRPS1 deficiency preferentially affects hearing, peripheral and central nervous system function, and ocular tissues. The exact tissue-selective intermediates remain incompletely defined, but the manifestations track with the severity of PRS-I activity loss.

evidence.snippet: In affected males, the PRS deficiency phenotypic spectrum ranges from severe congenital profound sensorineural hearing loss, intellectual disability, delayed motor development, and progressive ophthalmologic involvement (retinal dystrophy and optic atrophy) to normal cognitive abilities and relatively later-onset, somewhat milder manifestations, such as mild sensorineural hearing loss, peripheral neuropathy, and gait ataxia.

nodes.description: Optic neuropathy and atrophy in Arts syndrome and CMTX5 subtypes.

evidence.snippet: Arts syndrome is an X-linked disorder characterized by mental retardation, early-onset hypotonia, ataxia, delayed motor development, hearing impairment, and optic atrophy.
Arts syndrome is characterized by early-onset hypotonia, ataxia, intellectual disability, sensorineural hearing impairment, progressive optic atrophy, and a tendency to develop infections.
The diagnosis of PRS deficiency is established in a male proband with suggestive findings and a hemizygous pathogenic variant in PRPS1 identified by molecular genetic testing.

the loss-of-function mutations result in X-linked nonsyndromic sensorineural deafness type 2 (DFN2), or in syndromic deafness including Arts syndrome and X-linked Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease-5 (CMTX5)

Both mutations result in a loss of phosphoribosyl pyrophosphate synthetase 1 activity, as was shown in silico by molecular modeling and was shown in vitro by phosphoribosyl pyrophosphate synthetase activity assays in erythrocytes and fibroblasts from patients.
Enzymatically, PRS-I activity was undetectable in the index subject, reduced in his less affected sister, and normal in his unaffected mother.

readouts.interpretation: Reduced PRS-I enzyme activity directly reports the proximal PRPS1 loss-of-function mechanism.

nodes.description: Loss-of-function PRPS1 variants reduce PRS-I enzyme activity. Residual activity helps determine severity across the DFN2, CMTX5, and Arts syndrome continuum, with undetectable activity in some severe presentations.

evidence.snippet: Both mutations result in a loss of phosphoribosyl pyrophosphate synthetase 1 activity, as was shown in silico by molecular modeling and was shown in vitro by phosphoribosyl pyrophosphate synthetase activity assays in erythrocytes and fibroblasts from patients.
Enzymatically, PRS-I activity was undetectable in the index subject, reduced in his less affected sister, and normal in his unaffected mother.

nodes.description: Peripheral neuropathy is central to CMTX5 and can also be demonstrated in severe Arts syndrome cases.

evidence.snippet: We have identified missense mutations at conserved amino acids in the PRPS1 gene on Xq22.3 in two families with a syndromic form of inherited peripheral neuropathy

moderate PRS-I deficiency (CMTX5) and severe PRS-I deficiency (Arts syndrome) present with peripheral or optic neuropathy, prelingual progressive sensorineural hearing loss, and central nervous system impairment

nodes.description: S-adenosylmethionine can replenish purine nucleotides, and combined S-adenosylmethionine plus nicotinamide riboside has been reported to further improve clinical phenotype and T-cell survival/function in Arts syndrome. This is an emerging disease-directed option that bypasses some PRPP-dependent reactions; evidence remains limited to case reports and small literature reviews.
nodes.id: Purine and NAD Pathway Precursor Supplementation

evidence.snippet: Treatment of a 3-year old boy with S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) replenished erythrocyte purine nucleotides of adenosine and guanosine, while SAM and nicotinamide riboside co-therapy further improved his clinical phenotype as well as T-cell survival and function.
All patients had stability or improvement of symptoms, suggesting that SAMe and NR can be a treatment option in Arts syndrome, though further studies are warranted.
purine replacement via S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) supplementation in patients with Arts syndrome appears to improve their condition
SAM could compensate for PRS-I deficiency

nodes.description: Severe Arts syndrome often includes recurrent severe respiratory infections early in life, contributing to progressive clinical decline.

evidence.snippet: Classically, affected males present with sensorineural hearing loss, optic atrophy, muscular hypotonia, developmental impairment, and recurrent severe respiratory infections early in life.
developmental delays, ataxia, hypotonia, and recurrent infections that can cause progressive clinical decline

nodes.description: Retinal dystrophy is part of progressive ophthalmologic involvement in PRPS1 deficiency and is prominent in some severe expanded-spectrum cases.

evidence.snippet: progressive ophthalmologic involvement (retinal dystrophy and optic atrophy)
Additional phenotypic features include macular coloboma-like lesions with retinal dystrophy, severe short stature and diabetes insipidus.

nodes.description: Sensorineural hearing loss is present across the PRPS1 deficiency continuum. It may be congenital or later-onset, prelingual or postlingual, progressive or non-progressive, and can also occur as isolated or milder hearing impairment in heterozygous females.

evidence.snippet: In affected males, the PRS deficiency phenotypic spectrum ranges from severe congenital profound sensorineural hearing loss, intellectual disability, delayed motor development, and progressive ophthalmologic involvement (retinal dystrophy and optic atrophy) to normal cognitive abilities and relatively later-onset, somewhat milder manifestations, such as mild sensorineural hearing loss, peripheral neuropathy, and gait ataxia.
Hearing loss in male patients with PRPS1 mutations is bilateral, moderate to profound, and can be prelingual or postlingual, progressive or non-progressive.
impaired purine biosynthesis, which is supported by the undetectable hypoxanthine in urine and the reduced uric acid levels in serum from patients.

readouts.interpretation: Reduced serum uric acid reports impaired purine biosynthesis downstream of PRPP deficiency.

nodes.description: Severe postnatal short stature is reported in expanded-spectrum PRPS1 deficiency, beyond the prenatal-onset intrauterine growth restriction.

evidence.reference_title: Prenatal growth restriction, retinal dystrophy, diabetes insipidus and white matter disease: expanding the spectrum of PRPS1-related disorders.
evidence.snippet: Additional phenotypic features include macular coloboma-like lesions with retinal dystrophy, severe short stature and diabetes insipidus.

nodes.description: Spastic quadriparesis/tetraparesis is reported in severe expanded-spectrum PRPS1 deficiency.

evidence.snippet: intrauterine growth restriction, dysmorphic facial features, severe intellectual disability and spastic quadraparesis.
impaired purine biosynthesis, which is supported by the undetectable hypoxanthine in urine and the reduced uric acid levels in serum from patients.

readouts.interpretation: Undetectable urinary hypoxanthine reports impaired purine biosynthesis downstream of PRPP deficiency.

nodes.description: White matter disease/leukoencephalopathy is described in severe expanded-spectrum PRPS1 deficiency.

evidence.snippet: Prenatal growth restriction, retinal dystrophy, diabetes insipidus and white matter disease: expanding the spectrum of PRPS1-related disorders.
